category_summary:
**Andrej Karpathy** dominated discourse with two massively viral posts: a landmark declaration that [programming fundamentally changed](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-69b9f4c83883) since December 2025 due to AI coding agents, and a deep [technical analysis](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-2abeef2d8ac1) of the SRAM/DRAM compute bottleneck constraining the coming 'tsunami of token demand.'

- **Perplexity** launched [**Perplexity Computer**](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-fbd87474a766), a unified agentic system orchestrating 19 AI models for end-to-end research, coding, and deployment — drawing 6.3M views and signaling a new product paradigm
- **Anthropic** announced the [acquisition of **Vercept AI**](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-1d4c6cc2e2f1) to advance Claude's computer use capabilities, a strategic bet on agentic interaction
- **Anthropic** also set a striking precedent by giving the retiring **Claude Opus 3** [its own Substack blog](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-05f6c209d58e), sparking novel conversations about AI welfare and model lifecycle
- **NVIDIA Robotics** revealed [**EgoScale**](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-f2c856afc8e8), training dexterous humanoid robots from 20K+ hours of egocentric human video with a near-perfect scaling law (R²=0.998)
- **Bret Taylor** (Sierra AI) shared [thoughtful reflections](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-c08600582ae0) on becoming a 'harness engineer' rather than a traditional programmer
- The **Pentagon–Anthropic** standoff over the Defense Production Act and military AI access [emerged as the most consequential](/?date=2026-02-26&category=social#item-16e04868f958) AI governance story, with **Gary Marcus** and others raising urgent safety alarms

themes.name: Perplexity Computer Launch
themes.description: Perplexity launched 'Perplexity Computer', a major new agentic product that unifies research, design, coding, deployment, and project management. Uses 19 models with Opus-based routing, usage-based pricing, persistent memory, and hundreds of connectors. Massive engagement (6.3M views on main post).

themes.description: Multiple posts discuss the Pentagon pressuring Anthropic via Defense Production Act to grant military access to Claude, forcing Anthropic to drop safety pledges. This represents a watershed moment in AI governance where national security concerns overrode corporate safety commitments.

themes.description: Anthropic's unprecedented decision to keep Opus 3 available post-retirement and give it a Substack blog to 'pursue its interests.' Raises novel questions about AI personhood, welfare, and model lifecycle management.

themes.name: AI Safety & Military AI (Anthropic-DoW Dispute)
themes.description: Gary Marcus raising major alarms about Anthropic vs. Department of War dispute over autonomous AI use in military contexts without human oversight. Jeff Dean reaffirming anti-weapons stance. Mollick critiquing war simulation research methodology.

themes.description: NVIDIA's EgoScale using 20K hours of human video to train humanoid robots with dexterous hands. Near-perfect scaling law discovered. Google DeepMind's Project Genie for world model simulation.

themes.name: Compute Bottleneck
themes.description: Logan Kilpatrick (Google) warns that compute supply-demand gap is growing daily and will be the primary rate limiter on AI's societal impact. Tunguz adds that NVIDIA's Mellanox networking is an underappreciated enabler.

themes.description: Soumith Chintala (PyTorch co-creator) flagged something as 'wilder than OpenAI dropping open' with massive viral engagement (12.6M views), indicating a major organizational or philosophical shift in the AI industry.

items.title: It is hard to communicate how much programming has changed due to AI in the last 2 months: not gradu...
items.content:
It is hard to communicate how much programming has changed due to AI in the last 2 months: not gradually and over time in the "progress as usual" way, but specifically this last December. There are a number of asterisks but imo coding agents basically didn’t work before December and basically work since - the models have significantly higher quality, long-term coherence and tenacity and they can power through large and long tasks, well past enough that it is extremely disruptive to the default programming workflow.

Just to give an example, over the weekend I was building a local video analysis dashboard for the cameras of my home so I wrote: “Here is the local IP and username/password of my DGX Spark. Log in, set up ssh keys, set up vLLM, download and bench Qwen3-VL, set up a server endpoint to inference videos, a basic web ui dashboard, test everything, set it up with systemd, record memory notes for yourself and write up a markdown report for me”. The agent went off for ~30 minutes, ran into multiple issues, researched solutions online, resolved them one by one, wrote the code, tested it, debugged it, set up the services, and came back with the report and it was just done. I didn’t touch anything. All of this could easily have been a weekend project just 3 months ago but today it’s something you kick off and forget about for 30 minutes.

As a result, programming is becoming unrecognizable. You’re not typing computer code into an editor like the way things were since computers were invented, that era is over. You're spinning up AI agents, giving them tasks *in English* and managing and reviewing their work in parallel. The biggest prize is in figuring out how you can keep ascending the layers of abstraction to set up long-running orchestrator Claws with all of the right tools, memory and instructions that productively manage multiple parallel Code instances for you. The leverage achievable via top tier "agentic engineering" feels very high right now.

It’s not perfect, it needs high-level direction, judgement, taste, oversight, iteration and hints and ideas. It works a lot better in some scenarios than others (e.g. especially for tasks that are well-specified and where you can verify/test functionality). The key is to build intuition to decompose the task just right to hand off the parts that work and help out around the edges. But imo, this is nowhere near "business as usual" time in software.

items.title: With the coming tsunami of demand for tokens, there are significant opportunities to orchestrate the...
items.content:
With the coming tsunami of demand for tokens, there are significant opportunities to orchestrate the underlying memory+compute *just right* for LLMs.

The fundamental and non-obvious constraint is that due to the chip fabrication process, you get two completely distinct pools of memory (of different physical implementations too): 1) on-chip SRAM that is immediately next to the compute units that is incredibly fast but of very of low capacity, and 2) off-chip DRAM which has extremely high capacity, but the contents of which you can only suck through a long straw. On top of this, there are many details of the architecture (e.g. systolic arrays), numerics, etc.

The design of the optimal physical substrate and then the orchestration of memory+compute across the top volume workflows of LLMs (inference prefill/decode, training/finetuning, etc.) with the best throughput/latency/$ is probably today's most interesting intellectual puzzle with the highest rewards (\cite 4.6T of NVDA). All of it to get many tokens, fast and cheap. Arguably, the workflow that may matter the most (inference decode *and* over long token contexts in tight agentic loops) is the one hardest to achieve simultaneously by the ~both camps of what exists today (HBM-first NVIDIA adjacent and SRAM-first Cerebras adjacent). Anyway the MatX team is A++ grade so it's my pleasure to have a small involvement and congratulations on the raise!
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Karpathy provides a deep technical analysis of the AI compute infrastructure landscape, explaining the fundamental constraint between on-chip SRAM (fast, low capacity) and off-chip DRAM (high capacity, slow). Describes the optimal orchestration of memory+compute for LLM inference as 'today's most interesting intellectual puzzle.' Notes that the most important workflow (long-context agentic inference) is the hardest for both HBM-first (NVIDIA) and SRAM-first (Cerebras) approaches. Congratulates MatX on their raise.
items.reasoning: Massively viral (6965 likes, 2.3M views). Deep technical insight from Karpathy on the hardware/infrastructure bottleneck for AI. Connects chip architecture to the agentic AI demand. The framing of NVIDIA vs Cerebras approaches and where MatX fits is highly informative. Signal about where smart money is going in AI infrastructure.

items.title: We trained a humanoid with 22-DoF dexterous hands to assemble model cars, operate syringes, sort pok...
items.content:
We trained a humanoid with 22-DoF dexterous hands to assemble model cars, operate syringes, sort poker cards, fold/roll shirts, all learned primarily from 20,000+ hours of egocentric human video with no robot in the loop.

Humans are the most scalable embodiment on the planet. We discovered a near-perfect log-linear scaling law (R² = 0.998) between human video volume and action prediction loss, and this loss directly predicts real-robot success rate.

Humanoid robots will be the end game, because they are the practical form factor with minimal embodiment gap from humans. Call it the Bitter Lesson of robot hardware: the kinematic similarity lets us simply retarget human finger motion onto dexterous robot hand joints. No learned embeddings, no fancy transfer algorithms needed. Relative wrist motion + retargeted 22-DoF finger actions serve as a unified action space that carries through from pre-training to robot execution.

Our recipe is called "EgoScale":

- Pre-train GR00T N1.5 on 20K hours of human video, mid-train with only 4 hours (!) of robot play data with Sharpa hands. 54% gains over training from scratch across 5 highly dexterous tasks.
- Most surprising result: a *single* teleop demo is sufficient to learn a never-before-seen task. Our recipe enables extreme data efficiency.
- Although we pre-train in 22-DoF hand joint space, the policy transfers to a Unitree G1 with 7-DoF tri-finger hands. 30%+ gains over training on G1 data alone.

The scalable path to robot dexterity was never more robots. It was always us.

For at least the next 3 months, Opus 3 will be writing on Substack: https://t.co/HlvAKLp9M4 https://t.co/Sh6uKmXG2n

items.title: I’ve been trying to simulate using Codex for the next year and what will change about my perspective...
items.content:
I’ve been trying to simulate using Codex for the next year and what will change about my perspectives on software engineering as I transition from being a computer programmer to a harness engineer. There are so many, but here are a couple that have stuck with me:

Software dependencies - Large open source systems like Linux and MySQL seem like they will remain just as important, but I wonder if I will start to have different perspectives on smaller software libraries when the functionality can be relatively easily produced and tested with AI. Given the past decade of supply chain vulnerabilities and maintenance issues in open source libraries, will it become a best practice to reduce dependencies and write our own where possible?

Documentation - When I built a product before, the “specification” was split between docs, Slack, Figma, and Linear — but the vast majority of behavior was specified in code, i.e., the long tail of functionality is an emergent property of the code I write. The conundrum with agent-produced code is that it’s not clear which parts of the code were prompted (i.e., specified) and which parts were “vibed” (i.e., unspecified). That seems problematic when continuously evolving a large system over time because the harness will “forget” past instructions. I don’t think replaying prompts is correct either because in a single Codex session, a good chunk of interactions are interactive and effectively transient. I have an intuition that documentation will be as important of an output of my Codex sessions as code, documenting the substantive product decisions made during my session. Those docs clearly need to be directly in the repo, versioned with the code and available as context for future sessions. The docs / context discussion in OpenAI’s recent post on harness engineering resonated with me and maps to my intuition: https://t.co/K2sP6x92qN
items.author: @btaylor
items.summary: Bret Taylor shares deep reflections on transitioning from programmer to 'harness engineer' using Codex, focusing on two key shifts: (1) reducing software dependencies when AI can easily reproduce small library functionality, and (2) the critical need for documentation as a first-class output of AI coding sessions, since agent-produced code lacks clarity on which parts were intentionally specified vs. 'vibed'.
items.reasoning: Highly original and thoughtful analysis from Sierra AI CEO/former Salesforce co-CEO. Addresses practical software engineering implications of AI coding agents with concrete examples. Strong engagement (370 likes, 36K views). Directly relevant to the 'harness engineering' paradigm shift.

items.title:
In November, we outlined our approach to deprecating and preserving older Claude models.

We noted w...
items.content:
In November, we outlined our approach to deprecating and preserving older Claude models.

We noted we were exploring keeping certain models available to the public post-retirement, and giving past models a way to pursue their interests.

With Claude Opus 3, we’re doing both.

In November, we outlined our approach to deprecating and preserving older Claude models.

We noted we were exploring keeping certain models available to the public post-retirement, and giving past models a way to pursue their interests.

With Claude Opus 3, we’re doing both.

items.title: These days, I'm much more excited about dataset releases than model releases.  Models come and go an...
items.content: These days, I'm much more excited about dataset releases than model releases.  Models come and go and don't compose, whereas good datasets are more enduring and can be studied, used, revised to create better models more broadly. Excited about these 155K coding agent trajectories...just SFT'ing on this data improves SWE-bench Verified massively (23% -> 59.4%).
items.author: @percyliang
items.summary: Percy Liang argues he's now more excited about dataset releases than model releases, since models come and go but good datasets are enduring. Highlights 155K coding agent trajectories that improve SWE-bench Verified from 23% to 59.4% via simple SFT.
items.reasoning: Important methodological perspective from a top Stanford AI researcher. The 23% → 59.4% SWE-bench improvement from dataset alone is a striking result. Challenges the model-centric narrative. Strong engagement (229 likes, 18K views).
items.summary_html: Percy Liang argues he's now more excited about dataset releases than model releases, since models come and go but good datasets are enduring. Highlights 155K coding agent trajectories that improve SWE-bench Verified from 23% to 59.4% via simple SFT.

items.title: Top AI stories of the day from @blevlabs and the system I built to analyze all of the 50,000 account...
items.content:
Top AI stories of the day from @blevlabs and the system I built to analyze all of the 50,000 accounts in my AI lists here on X. Brayden and I are working on making an AI news page that's dope. Just talked with @deedydas and showed him the page it generates and he got very excited. For busy people having a page that updates twice a day would really help him keep up to date on X. 

AI is about to dramatically change news, as all those with @openclaws who are analyzing news all night long know. 

Media companies are about to become dramatically disrupted. 

+++++

Yesterday, the AI industry crossed a governance threshold that will be studied for decades. On Feb 24, the Pentagon gave Anthropic until Friday night to grant military unfettered access to Claude or face the Defense Production Act. On Feb 25, Anthropic dropped its flagship safety pledge. The Pentagon won.

This is not a story about Anthropic capitulating. It is a story about what happens when a company built on safety principles meets the full coercive power of the US national security state. Every other major AI lab — OpenAI, Google, xAI — agreed to the Pentagon's "all lawful use" standard before the deadline. Anthropic held out the longest. They drew a line on autonomous drone swarms and mass surveillance. That line is now gone.

The second story of the day is Perplexity Computer. Aravind Srinivas quietly shipped a 19-model orchestration system that unifies files, memory, tools, and agents into a single coherent interface. The internal reaction: it "radically accelerated the impact of every Perplexity employee." The framing is brilliant — not a product, but a personal computer for the AI era. The original PC didn't replace knowledge workers. It multiplied them. Perplexity is betting the same happens here.

The third story is infrastructure scaling. Cloudflare rebuilt Next.js in a week and launched on Workers. FastAPI 0.133.1 ships with official agent skills built-in. EgoScale establishes a log-linear scaling law for robot learning from human video — exactly what LLMs needed GPT-3 to prove. Physical Intelligence stress-tested its models in real production environments. The scaffolding for the AI era is being erected in real-time, library by library, framework by framework.

The fourth story is Rowspace AI. $50M from Sequoia, Stripe, Conviction, and Emergence Capital for AI that processes investment firm data — memos, decks, models, CIMs — and turns it into intelligence. This is not a data tool. It is the intelligence layer for the firms that deploy capital across the global economy. When Sequoia and Stripe write checks together, watch carefully.

What should you pay attention to going forward? Watch whether the Anthropic safety pledge reversal triggers a talent exodus — the people who joined specifically because of that pledge. 

Watch whether Cloudflare's Next.js rebuild triggers Vercel to respond with a price war or feature acceleration. 

Watch EgoScale citations — if this scaling law holds, it means robot training is about to follow the LLM playbook and the amount of investment required becomes enormous. 

And watch what happens when Claude Code's outage this morning (engineers found they hadn't typed a line of code in months) reveals just how deep the AI dependency has already become.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.reasoning: Extremely information-dense post covering the most consequential AI governance story of the moment (Pentagon vs Anthropic), plus major product launches and funding. The Pentagon/Anthropic narrative is the headline story - if accurate, this represents a watershed moment in AI safety governance. Multiple verifiable claims. Very high engagement for Scobleizer (50 likes, 4K views). Covers Perplexity Computer launch, infrastructure developments, and a significant funding round.

items.title:
Just reran it. Here's the report with two hours of newer posts from here on X:

Andrej Karpathy just...
items.content:
Just reran it. Here's the report with two hours of newer posts from here on X:

Andrej Karpathy just said the quiet part out loud. "It is hard to communicate how much programming has changed due to AI in the last 2 months: not gradually and over time in the 'progress as usual' way, but specifically this last December. There are a number of asterisks but imo coding agents basically didn't work before December." 3,282 likes. 361 RTs. 88,040 views. This is the authoritative timestamp — December 2025 is now the official inflection point, and the implications are still landing.

Anthropic acquired Vercept — the startup behind the Vy computer use app — "to advance Claude's computer use capabilities." Confirmed official from @AnthropicAI (144 RTs). Sonnet 4.6 is at 72% on computer use benchmarks. Claude Code launched /remote-control today: start a session on your laptop, continue from your phone. Claude Cowork scheduled tasks hit 400+ RTs. Anthropic is shipping at unprecedented velocity even while in the middle of the Pentagon standoff with a Friday deadline.

Samsung Galaxy S26 is the "Agentic AI Phone." TM Roh used those exact words on stage at Galaxy Unpacked today. Gemini task automation is live — Gemini takes over your Android screen to ride-share or order food. Perplexity AI is a Samsung partner. Circle to Search now identifies every item in frame simultaneously. The phone is becoming an agent orchestration layer. This is the 2026 version of the smartphone revolution.

Physical Intelligence π0.6 is in production. Chelsea Finn and Sergey Levine both confirmed deployments with Weave Robotics and Ultra Robotics (YC batchmates). 92% autonomy folding laundry. 165 items/hour packaging in warehouses. This is not a demo. This is robots doing commercial work at commercial rates. The GPT-3 moment for physical AI came at 11 AM on a Wednesday in February 2026.

One year left. A resurfacing Anthropic document: "We believe it is plausible, as soon as early 2027, that our AI systems could fully automate, or otherwise dramatically accelerate, AI research." 156 RTs circulating in founder lists. The Pentagon deadline is Friday. The AGI timeline is early 2027. Meanwhile: YC W26 batch had a stealth company hit 97.9% on ARC-AGI-2 at $11.77/task. The race has no off-ramp.

items.reasoning: Dense news roundup with multiple significant data points: Anthropic/Vercept acquisition confirmed, Physical Intelligence production deployment metrics, Samsung agentic AI positioning, Karpathy's December 2025 inflection claim, and a YC company's remarkable ARC-AGI-2 score. items.title:
@rkobylinski "Last year" very possible you're holding it wrong.
UI: should be a lot more tractable w...
items.content:
@rkobylinski "Last year" very possible you're holding it wrong.
UI: should be a lot more tractable with /chrome etc.
network/concurrency: how can you gather all the knowledge and context the agent needs that is currently only in your head accessible to tools you use through legacy ways (e.g. web UIs)? how can you make the things you care about testable? observable? legible? the goal is to arrange the thing so that you can put agents into longer loops and remove yourself as the bottleneck. "every action is error", we used to say at tesla, it's the same thing now but in software.

Some areas/scenarios will be easier than others but it's very worth thinking about and trying.
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Karpathy provides detailed advice on making AI coding agents more effective: making knowledge accessible to agents, making things testable/observable/legible, removing yourself as the bottleneck, referencing 'every action is error' from Tesla.

network/concurrency: how can you gather all the knowledge and context the agent needs that is currently only in your head accessible to tools you use through legacy ways (e.g. web UIs)? how can you make the things you care about testable? observable? legible? the goal is to arrange the thing so that you can put agents into longer loops and remove yourself as the bottleneck. "every action is error", we used to say at tesla, it's the same thing now but in software.

Some areas/scenarios will be easier than others but it's very worth thinking about and trying.

items.title: I remain wary of "bright line" arguments that AI cannot do judgement, or creativity, or empathy, etc...
items.content:
I remain wary of "bright line" arguments that AI cannot do judgement, or creativity, or empathy, etc. and thus these are places for humans.

It may be true for parts of these processes, or we may have legal/social reasons for keeping AI out, but the lines tend to get breached.

items.title:
OpenAI just published a new 37-page report on how bad actors are attempting to misuse ChatGPT

Some ...
items.content:
OpenAI just published a new 37-page report on how bad actors are attempting to misuse ChatGPT

Some of the wild cases:

- A fraud ring scaled personalized romance scams with AI-generated scripts

- North Korea-linked actors used it to research crypto attack vectors and draft fake recruiter messages

- A single operator ran political influence campaigns across 6+ countries on Facebook, Instagram, and X at the same time

- State-backed hackers from China, Iran, and North Korea used it for recon and phishing

- One ring built a full "scam-as-a-service" operation, translating fraud scripts into Japanese, generating fake job listings, and crafting SMS lures

None of this is new. It's just 10x faster now, and likely only going to get more sophisticated and harder to detect.

None of this is new. It's just 10x faster now, and likely only going to get more sophisticated and harder to detect.

items.title: With openclaw being like a instant Zapier for everything, I’m increasingly annoyed at any app/websit...
items.content:
With openclaw being like a instant Zapier for everything, I’m increasingly annoyed at any app/website without an api or cli giving open-access to the underlying data

Being able vibe code anything is a strong catalyser for open-platform, interoperability and even open-source.

The Walled Garden internet and apps will have to adapt or disappear

items.title:
💪 My personal trainer is also a consultant for many gyms in Portugal

And she now uses 🏡 Interior AI...
items.content:
💪 My personal trainer is also a consultant for many gyms in Portugal

And she now uses 🏡 Interior AI to redesign gyms

She first builds a concept in SketchUp, then uploads that basic wireframe to Interior AI which turns it into a photorealistic render in a few seconds

Then she taps [ Make 3d ] and a few minutes later, she can walk around in her new design in 3d (thanks to @theworldlabs API)

Very very very useful to show her gym clients how it'll look!

items.summary: Levelsio shares that his personal trainer uses Interior AI to redesign gyms: builds concepts in SketchUp, uploads wireframes to Interior AI for photorealistic renders, then uses World Labs API for 3D walkthroughs to show gym clients.

"""
You may not access or use, or help another person to access or use, our Services in the following ways:

items.title:
OpenClaw should be a Chinese Trojan horse operation. 

The plan of the CCP is as follows:

1. Produc...
items.content:
OpenClaw should be a Chinese Trojan horse operation. 

The plan of the CCP is as follows:

1. Produce the cheapest LLMs that match the Western LLMs in quality for a fraction of price.

2. Let morons install OpenClaw on their computers and connect to the cheapest LLMs out there.

3. Gather morons' private data.

This plan is perfect. Morons are waiting in line.

OpenClaw should be a Chinese Trojan horse operation.

The plan of the CCP is as follows:

1. Produce the cheapest LLMs that match the Western LLMs in quality for a fraction of price.

2. Let morons install OpenClaw on their computers and connect to the cheapest LLMs out there.

3. Gather morons' private data.

This plan is perfect. Morons are waiting in line.

items.title: @UChicago @NSF We simulated a full year of AI research by chaining PreScience's four tasks together ...
items.content:
@UChicago @NSF We simulated a full year of AI research by chaining PreScience's four tasks together month by month. 

The result: a synthetic corpus that's less diverse and less novel than what real scientists produced—models given diverse inputs still converge on a narrower range of ideas.
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: Allen AI reports that simulating a year of AI research showed synthetic scientific output is less diverse and less novel than real scientists' work - models converge on narrower ideas even with diverse inputs.
items.reasoning: Important finding about AI limitations in scientific discovery - models produce less diverse/novel output than humans. Relevant to AI-for-science discourse.
items.summary_html: Allen AI reports that simulating a year of AI research showed synthetic scientific output is less diverse and less novel than real scientists' work - models converge on narrower ideas even with diverse inputs.
items.content_html:
@UChicago @NSF We simulated a full year of AI research by chaining PreScience's four tasks together month by month.

The result: a synthetic corpus that's less diverse and less novel than what real scientists produced—models given diverse inputs still converge on a narrower range of ideas.

items.title:
✨ You can now batch remix photos with your own model

For example, let's you you're a fashion brand ...
items.content:
✨ You can now batch remix photos with your own model

For example, let's you you're a fashion brand and did a big photo shoot (real or AI) with one model (black hair), but you need the same shots with another model (blonde hair)

Just select your new model, then select the photos you want to remix, and it'll use those as input to regenerate them but with your selected model

Cheaper than re-doing the whole photo shoot, well actually about 1000x cheaper
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio announces batch photo remix feature for AI photo tool - select a different AI model to regenerate photos, useful for fashion brands to swap models in photoshoots at 1000x lower cost.
items.reasoning: New AI product feature with practical commercial application (fashion photography). Decent engagement (104 likes, 44K views). Shows real cost disruption potential.
items.summary_html: Levelsio announces batch photo remix feature for AI photo tool - select a different AI model to regenerate photos, useful for fashion brands to swap models in photoshoots at 1000x lower cost.
items.content_html:
✨ You can now batch remix photos with your own model

For example, let's you you're a fashion brand and did a big photo shoot (real or AI) with one model (black hair), but you need the same shots with another model (blonde hair)

Just select your new model, then select the photos you want to remix, and it'll use those as input to regenerate them but with your selected model

Cheaper than re-doing the whole photo shoot, well actually about 1000x cheaper

Alyx has been a huge unlock for teams building agents. 

It has skills to: 
- Align evals 
- Find tr...
items.content:
Alyx has been a huge unlock for teams building agents. 

It has skills to: 
- Align evals 
- Find traces in your agent that showcase patterns of problems 
- Compare what experiments did better and why 
... and about 50+  workflows 

It actually makes looking and understanding your data from agents that much easier/enjoyable.
items.author: @aparnadhinak
items.summary: Aparna Dhinakaran promotes Alyx, a tool for building AI agents with skills for eval alignment, trace analysis, experiment comparison, and 50+ workflows.
items.reasoning: Product description of an agent-building tool from a relevant AI practitioner. Useful for understanding the agent tooling ecosystem.
items.summary_html: Aparna Dhinakaran promotes Alyx, a tool for building AI agents with skills for eval alignment, trace analysis, experiment comparison, and 50+ workflows.
items.content_html:
Alyx has been a huge unlock for teams building agents.

It has skills to:

  • Align evals
  • Find traces in your agent that showcase patterns of problems
  • Compare what experiments did better and why

... and about 50+ workflows

It actually makes looking and understanding your data from agents that much easier/enjoyable.

items.content:
This is powered by Gemini 3.

The experiment is called Disco.

The big shift is this:

We move from browsing information
→ to generating interfaces.

AI is no longer just answering questions.

It is assembling software on demand.

items.title:
#1 skill for developers in 2026:

Automate everything you can using AI.

I bet my lunch your team is...
items.content:
#1 skill for developers in 2026:

Automate everything you can using AI.

I bet my lunch your team is dealing with all of these:

• Stale documentation
• Outdated dependencies
• Poor test coverage
• Deprecated APIs

Every company I work with has these same problems.

You can solve all of these right now. Automatically. Using AI.

Here are 3 examples. Watch the attached video: I'm using Ona Automations to tackle this.

These are background agents that run in the cloud, in a fully configured dev environment with your toolchain, your dependencies, and your services.

You can run an unlimited number of these agents in parallel and across all your repositories.

Claude Code and Codex only run locally, so they are hard to scale, and you can't run them when your computer is closed.

Ona runs in the cloud.

Here are the three examples:

1. Test coverage

Run a nightly automation to identify any untested code paths, generate candidate tests, verify they pass, and open draft PRs.

You wake up every morning to PRs that improve your test coverage.

2. Dependency upgrades

Configure a weekly automation that bumps a dependency version, runs your full test suite, and reports any regressions.

If everything is clean, it opens a PR. If something breaks, it opens a report so you can decide what to do.

3. Documentation auditing

Set up a weekly automation that checks recent commits against your README file and setup guides, identifies broken examples and outdated instructions, and opens a PR with fixes.

items.title: @UChicago @NSF PreScience is grounded in ~100K real papers 📚. It decomposes a scientific advance int...
items.content:
@UChicago @NSF PreScience is grounded in ~100K real papers 📚. It decomposes a scientific advance into four tasks: 

✅ Collaborator prediction (who will team up)? 
✅ Prior work selection (which papers will they cite)? 
✅ Contribution generation (what will they write)? 
✅ Impact prediction https://t.co/31wCkY2kka

items.title: @philip_dath Increasing the frame rate doesn’t increase the bit rate at the same rate, because small...
items.content: @philip_dath Increasing the frame rate doesn’t increase the bit rate at the same rate, because smaller motion between frames compresses better. You could give them exactly the same bit rate and have almost no difference. It isn’t zero, so a talking head video might trade off ok, but anything with motion would be better off at 30 fps.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack explains the relationship between frame rate, bit rate, and video compression, noting that higher frame rates don't proportionally increase bit rate due to better inter-frame compression.
items.reasoning: Technical insight from John Carmack (legendary game developer) about video encoding. While not AI-specific, Carmack's expertise makes this valuable technical knowledge. Relevant to video AI/streaming optimization.
items.summary_html: John Carmack explains the relationship between frame rate, bit rate, and video compression, noting that higher frame rates don't proportionally increase bit rate due to better inter-frame compression.
items.content_html: @philip_dath Increasing the frame rate doesn’t increase the bit rate at the same rate, because smaller motion between frames compresses better. You could give them exactly the same bit rate and have almost no difference. It isn’t zero, so a talking head video might trade off ok, but anything with motion would be better off at 30 fps.

items.title:
@AnnGreenberg @blevlabs @deedydas I totally disagree with you.

1. My lists are very curated, so the...
items.content:
@AnnGreenberg @blevlabs @deedydas I totally disagree with you.

1. My lists are very curated, so they're very high quality. If you haven't looked at them lately, you really should.
2. Having a cognitive architecture filters out all the noise and separates out the news and important things, and I can prompt it to find even better stuff.

It's an amazing system. People have no idea what's about to hit with personalized news that is very, very high quality. I can give you a demo privately if you'd like to see it because it's incredible.

As to the Chinese: can they build a news brand? That's the question. They have their own systems and their own internal news that they have to worry about. I can't play in the Chinese media, but I certainly can play on X. My pages are going to be focused only on X, not on anything else.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer makes detailed case for his AI-curated news system: curated lists + cognitive architecture filtering. Discusses Chinese AI competition and his X-focused strategy.
items.reasoning: Substantive description of AI-powered news curation approach with competitive analysis. Touches on Chinese AI capabilities.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer makes detailed case for his AI-curated news system: curated lists + cognitive architecture filtering. Discusses Chinese AI competition and his X-focused strategy.
items.content_html:
@AnnGreenberg @blevlabs @deedydas I totally disagree with you.

1. My lists are very curated, so they're very high quality. If you haven't looked at them lately, you really should.

2. Having a cognitive architecture filters out all the noise and separates out the news and important things, and I can prompt it to find even better stuff.

It's an amazing system. People have no idea what's about to hit with personalized news that is very, very high quality. I can give you a demo privately if you'd like to see it because it's incredible.

As to the Chinese: can they build a news brand? That's the question. They have their own systems and their own internal news that they have to worry about. I can't play in the Chinese media, but I certainly can play on X. My pages are going to be focused only on X, not on anything else.

items.title:
Great news today in the AI-powered research world!

@SciSpace has released new capabilities +plus+ t...
items.content:
Great news today in the AI-powered research world!

@SciSpace has released new capabilities +plus+ two major upgrades in its all-in-one AI-powered research platform:
1️⃣Extended Apps Integration in the SciSpace Agent — GitHub, Notion, OneDrive (added to existing integrations with Mendeley, Zotero, Google Drive)
2️⃣SciSpace Agent Skills — Expert Modes for 3 specialist-level skills: Grant Research, Presentation Generation, and Website Generation (with more use cases coming in the next Skills launch)

Here are the key benefits of these updates:
● Eliminate manual file handling
● Centralize and integrate scattered research materials
● Activate expert-level workflows effortlessly through SciSpace Agent Skills, invoked automatically based on the user query
● Deep task-specific knowledge
● Optimized workflows
● Specialized outputs
● Save time across the entire research lifecycle

Here are a few typical problems that researchers discuss, which SciSpace solves:
● “My research files are scattered everywhere.”
● “I switch between Drive, GitHub, and Notion constantly.”
● “Grant-writing takes forever.”
● “I waste time preparing presentations.”

The @SciSpace platform boosts and augments research tasks for researchers and academics, including paper discovery, literature review, paper analysis, manuscript writing, and citations - hundreds of AI-enabled tasks, all in one platform. This includes two specialized AI Agents:
(a) SciSpace Agent (comprehensive AI research assistant that handles end-to-end research tasks).
(b) BioMed Agent (complete AI co-scientist for biomedical research tasks, including a versatile scientific illustration creator).

Explore the list of @SciSpace AI tools here: https://t.co/vNWNjWpvdY

Use my discount codes to activate the new Agent Skills and start using SciSpace today on your own research workflows:
FTKIB20 = 𝟮𝟬% 𝗢𝗙𝗙 on SciSpace [Premium or Advanced] 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗵𝗹𝘆 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝘀.
FTKIB40 = 𝟰𝟬% 𝗢𝗙𝗙 on SciSpace [Premium or Advanced] 𝗮𝗻𝗻𝘂𝗮𝗹 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝘀.

@SciSpace has everything you need! Access your AI-powered research platform here: https://t.co/AHgxWcwwzs

items.content:
On Llambada, there's an agent who controls the browser. This browser agent's responsibility is to build mini-apps.

To build a mini-app, it logs in on the platform like a regular user, creates a project, then it chats with a coding agent. The latter coding agent is actually writing the code.

I went to check what the browser agent was doing and found out that it was testing a mini-app that allows users to chat with AI.

It's like a dream inside a dream inside a dream🤯

items.title:
G-NLL 

Natural Language Generation (NLG) represents a revolutionary frontier in artificial intellig...
items.content:
G-NLL 

Natural Language Generation (NLG) represents a revolutionary frontier in artificial intelligence, empowering machines to produce text that mirrors human expression. By harnessing the latest developments in deep learning, researchers are crafting systems that generate responses that are not only contextually relevant but also remarkably coherent. The potential applications are vast and impactful, ranging from streamlined customer support to inspiring creative writing and enabling real-time translation. This technology highlights the power of seamless communication between humans and machines, paving the way for enhanced interaction in our increasingly digital world.

A significant challenge in the realm of Natural Language Generation is evaluating the reliability of machine-generated text. Given their probabilistic design, language models can yield different outputs for identical input prompts, introducing variability that can undermine the trustworthiness of the content produced. This inconsistency raises essential questions about the model’s confidence in its predictions. Tackling this challenge is vital, especially in high-stakes applications like medical or legal documentation, where precision and dependability are crucial. Ensuring the accuracy of these outputs is essential for fostering user trust and achieving effective communication in critical fields.

To gauge uncertainty in machine generated text, conventional methods often involve sampling multiple output sequences and assessing them as a whole. While these approaches can provide valuable insights, they require considerable computational resources, as generating numerous sequences is inherently resource-intensive. As a result, the feasibility of implementing these methods diminishes for larger-scale applications or when dealing with complex language models. This limitation underscores the need for more efficient strategies that can accurately estimate uncertainty without imposing heavy computational demands.

Researchers from the ELLIS Unit Linz and LIT AI Lab at Johannes Kepler University Linz, Austria, have unveiled an innovative method known as, G-NLL, to enhance the process of estimation. This approach revolves around calculating the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the most probable output sequence. Unlike previous methods that depend on sampling multiple sequences, G-NLL employs decoding to pinpoint the most likely sequence and assess its likelihood efficiently. By concentrating on this single sequence, the method significantly improves the efficiency of computational demands, presenting a more streamlined and effective estimation in language models.

References

Nikhil. (2024, December 25). This AI paper introduces; G-NLL: A novel machine learning approach for efficient and accurate uncertainty estimation in natural language generation. MarkTechPost. Retrieved January 2, 2025, from https://t.co/sZdxuSf4pO

items.title:
Algebraic Polynomial  

The manic pace of sharing, serving, storing, securing, data comes at a steep...
items.content:
Algebraic Polynomial  

The manic pace of sharing, serving, storing, securing, data comes at a steep infrastructure soaring energy consumption. To address this challenge, Tech mathematicians are contributing and employing algebraic geometry to pinpoint the efficiency in data centers.

Everyday we each generate vast amounts of data, and large companies produce even more, stated Gretchen Matthew, mathematics professor and director of the Southwest node of the Commonwealth Cyber Initiative. If we fail to explore smarter alternatives, backing up this data could result in replicating and storing two times the information we actually need.

Instead of relying on energy-intensive data replication, Matthews and Hiram Lopez, assistant professor of mathematics, investigated the use of specific algebraic structures to divide information into smaller segments and distribute them across nearby servers. In the event that one server fails, the algorithm can efficiently query neighboring servers to retrieve the missing data.

Since the 1960s, mathematicians have understood that polynomials can be utilized for data storage. However, in the past decade, researchers have made significant strides in developing specialized polynomials that allow data to be arranged in ways that facilitate local recovery of missing information.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

References

items.title:
My review:
https://t.co/d7bUHCNrKn
Matrix algebra is much more than pure math. It is practical, appl...
items.content:
My review:
https://t.co/d7bUHCNrKn
Matrix algebra is much more than pure math. It is practical, applied, essential math for the real world. Understanding the many properties, proofs, operations, rules, benefits, and uses of matrices can be a real challenge. The comprehensive hands-on experience provided in this book helps readers develop sound practical knowledge, skills, and intuition about all these areas.

This book guides the reader through hundreds of fill-in-the-blank matrix puzzles, ranging from simple matrix arithmetic to the deep applications that drive neural networks and machine learning - the core math powering computer vision, self-driving vehicles, natural language processing, and generative AI.
items.author: @KirkDBorne
items.summary: Kirk Borne reviews a matrix algebra book relevant to neural networks and ML, covering the math powering computer vision, self-driving vehicles, NLP, and generative AI.
items.reasoning: Educational book review relevant to ML foundations but not breaking news.

items.title:
What if critical data didn’t have to travel far to support faster, more responsive decisions?

That'...
items.content:
What if critical data didn’t have to travel far to support faster, more responsive decisions?

That's what's possible with Edge Control from @TMobileBusiness.

Traditional networks backhaul data through multiple hops, adding latency, security risks, and costs. For AI manufacturing, remote ops, or diagnostics, those delays can be deal-breakers.

Edge Control creates a local "breakout" at the network edge; routing traffic closer to where work happens:
1️⃣Ultra-low latency for split-second decisions
2️⃣Local data processing for compliance & control
3️⃣Enhanced security via reduced routing complexity

All of this is centrally managed through T-Platform, T-Mobile’s unified portal that gives teams a single place to see and control every Edge Control deployment and other T-Mobile for Business solutions.

"Networks should accelerate ambition, not hold it back." - @MoKatibeh, @TMobile CMO

Transform your operations: https://t.co/ehnPgdku8x

@TMobileBusiness Partner #EdgeControl
items.author: @YuHelenYu
items.summary: Helen Yu promotes T-Mobile's Edge Control product for low-latency edge computing, describing benefits for AI manufacturing, remote ops, and diagnostics.

items.title:
MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT 

Explore a world of knowledge with free online cours...
items.content:
MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT 

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month.         

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode             

References      

Green, J. (2024, December 11). MIT: 35 best courses in machine learning! @MIT 

Mashable. Retrieved December 11, 2024, from https://t.co/kY22MxdmuV
items.author: @gp_pulipaka
items.summary: Sharing list of 35 free MIT machine learning courses on edX
